speaker: interviewer
text: With Edith Millman. Edith, would you continue the story that you were telling us about how you developed a relationship with a German--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, don't say relationship [LAUGHS] with German, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. I stand corrected.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. Well, as I said, he gave me his phone number. And he said if I ever needed him, you know, to-- to call. And a couple of weeks later, my mother came to me, completely out of breath. And she says, she heard that this one woman who was supposedly a Volksdeutsche, is spreading rumors, and saying-- telling everybody that she is Jewish. Not me, because I guess she didn't know about me. I don't know. But that she's Jewish, and my mother is afraid that, you know, somebody will-- she will have the Gestapo come.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and they have nowhere to go. Nothing is prepared in another place. They don't know where to go or hide. And so I called-- I-- I got on the phone, and I called this Lieutenant Ostrus. And I said, you know, you once said that if I need you, you would help. And I think I need you. And so he said, OK. I'll be right there. And he took his bike, and came on his bike. And he-- and my mother was still with me at the railroad station. And I introduced my mother as my cousin, which I guess I must have told him before that I'm here with my cousin. But I-- he always felt that I was that poor half-German girl who was out of her milieu, so to speak, who really comes from Silesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, you know, I don't have anybody here. I don't know anybody. So I-- anyway I said to him, you know, I am not from here. Nobody really knows-- knows us. And there is this woman who is accusing my cousin and me of being Jewish. And he looked, and he said, oh, that's stupid. How can-- you know, the stupidity. But, you know, the chutzpah now, when I think of it, to go to a German and tell him that I'm being accused of being Jewish, and can he help me. You know, like I can--

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he says, who is doing it? And I said, you know, this one woman. So he said, come on. I go with you. And all three of us, he with this bicycle, and we went there. And he lived-- she lived on the second floor. And it was in this spring-- must have been spring of '43 or '44, I forgot. But it was springtime. And he-- so we went up, and the windows were open. And he-- and we went in there. And he started to say to her, in German, what makes you think that these two ladies are Jewish? And how-- you know, and she doesn't understand a word of German. But she was always pretending, maybe she had some German blood in her or somehow. But she was always the Volksdeutsche. And she didn't understand. So he told me to translate. So I really translated that good. I mean I really scared the daylights out of her. But I didn't really have to. Because he-- he really said, if he hears once more of her making stupid rumors like that, he personally is going to take her to the Gestapo, and have her arrested for impersonating a Volksdeutsche, when she doesn't even speak German.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he scared her. And she was all over. She started to kiss my mother's hand. And she-- you know, she was, oh, she was like a worm. She was so scared. And so-- and then we left, and-- with that threat that if he hears once more that she is making some new rumors. When we came down, his bicycle was covered with flowers. Because the Poles, you know, they saw a German come with us, a German officer. And as I mentioned, the windows were open. And all that yelling, and they all hated her. Because apparently she was a woman in the neighborhood who always would denounce people of any infraction, not only Jews but other things. So-- so that's about it. And soon after that, I think he was-- he was sent to Yugoslavia or somewhere. But, you know, when I tell people that I went to a German officer to tell him, I'm being accused of being Jewish, help me, it's quite-- so I had that story. And I-- I mean I-- there's so many. The one-- one story I do want to say is-- or tell, is when I-- I wanted to make some extra money. And so on weekends, I got an extra job selling tickets. The whole line there, where the railroad was, was little resort towns. And people would come for a weekends or come for just a Saturday or Sunday, and-- and spend money, get drunk, and then on the way back when they were buying the tickets, and the ticket was-- I forgot what it was. But let's say it was like $1, the equivalent of a $1 or $0.80, so they would throw a dollar. And I would get-- you know, and not take the-- the-- the money, the $0.20. No, leave it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So not only did I make extra money getting paid, but I also made extra money from what was left from the tickets. And one time, there was a girl that I went to school with in this-- the Catholic school, in the parochial school that I mentioned. And she wasn't a good friend of mine, but she was not an enemy. She was just a classmate. And she came, and she recognized me. She was with some guys. And she recognized me. But she didn't want to say immediately, you know, that she recognized me. But she even asked some stupid questions. Anyway I-- I felt a little uncomfortable about it. But then I knew that-- I wasn't-- I never had any real dealings with her, and she wasn't an enemy. We weren't that friendly. But she was just a classmate.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I told-- I had a boyfriend at the time. And I told him that I-- about it. And he said, no. You're not going to go back there. And I said, oh, no. She wouldn't denounce me. But anyway, he said he would come and look out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Jewish, the boyfriend?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And he was-- well, actually he was kind of half-Jewish. But he wasn't brought up as a Jew. And so-- so he went with me. And he said, he would warn me if he sees something. And sure enough, the next week, he did. I was selling tickets in an office. And I heard some commotion on the side. And I couldn't see. Because there was a wall. But there was a-- a German who came. And he started-- he kind of knelt down, right in front of this little window where I was selling. And he-- with his gun drawn, like knelt on one-- one leg, I guess, and below the window with a gun drawn. And the next thing I knew was this boy, and another German were fighting. And then somehow they got separated. And the shot rang out. And my boyfriend was killed. And so I-- I saw-- I saw that. That was-- was a very traumatic moment. But there was a lot of confusion. And the-- the thing is, kind of strange thing is, when we were walking towards my job, I found a penny, and I gave him-- I mean a grosz, a Polish penny. And I gave it to him, and he wanted to give it back to me, and so on. And then I finally gave it, no, you keep it for good luck. And when he was killed, I ran out. And there was all this commotion, many people. Brain was just oozing out. And-- but his fist was clenched. And I opened it. There was a penny in it. And that's all I saw. And then I ran, and then I didn't come back to that place of work.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Russians were coming in already. So-- one more? It's up to you, Edith.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one time when-- where I was subletting the room, I-- I always felt pretty safe. I didn't think that the administrator of the building knew that I was Jewish. The building used to belong to Jews, but they were killed. So anyway, I met a friend of mine, a boy that I knew in the ghetto. I saw him in the street. And he had no-- no place to live. He escaped from the ghetto with his mother and sister. And I knew there was an empty apartment in the building. So I told him about it, and he-- he got the apartment. But about three days later, this administrator came, knocked on the door and came. I let him in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he started to tell me about Jews and that he's not an anti-Semite. And-- you know, like preparing something. And I pretended I didn't understand him. And then he came right out with it. And he said, I think you're Jewish. But I'm going to protect you, if you agree to be my mistress. And here I was, you know, a young girl. And I didn't know now what to do. Should I become his mistress, or what? And so I was still stunned.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- [LAUGHS] he must have been in his late 50s maybe. And then he came, and he came at me, and he was smiling. All I can remember is his half-rotten teeth, you know. And I saw-- and-- and I think up to that point I didn't know what to do. You know, if I should submit, or-- or what. And but when I saw these half-rotten teeth and and that grin, I just slapped him so hard in the face. And I started to cuss in good Polish cuss words.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, you so-in-so, how dare you? Aren't you ashamed? And I said-- then I said, if you don't get out of here immediately and leave me alone, I go to your wife-- he had a wife there and about four kids-- and tell her about-- about it. And not only that, I said, but I am going to go to the Gestapo, and tell them that you are willing to hide a Jew for some favors. [LAUGHTER] He got so scared. So he ran. I never had trouble with him anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my-- I told my friend, and they moved out. Because they realized that he must have you know, got-- got suspicious from once I recommended the family. And he probably knew that they were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, were there any Poles who helped through this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I helped in so far that I gave them the false ID cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were helping. You-- you maintained contact?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I maintained contact, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In some-- With the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, with-- with the guy that was in the underground. So I guess in-- in a way you can say that, yes. I also was able to get-- give a job to another woman, a girl, who was at the switchboard. And then we knew about certain trains that were going, important trains. Because they always had to clear the tracks for some trains. So I knew-- so then when we knew that some important trains were coming, I would call that guy, Czesiak, and he would-- I don't know what they did with that information. But I don't know if it was in time to--

speaker: interviewer
text: You would inform them when these trains were coming?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I don't know if it was important or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had one contact then to the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm. Well, actually two. There was another guy that also helped me with some papers. And so I-- I think both of them were involved. I don't think-- I don't know if they were in the same group. I didn't want to know. Because I, frankly, I didn't know if I could take torture. So I didn't want to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Polish people who knew that you or your parents were Jewish who helped you? You talked about a prostitute with your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah, well let's see, the first-- [SIGHS] on the first paper, the first papers, the second papers; there were people who-- who are willing to help, you know. And I think, like, a woman who would warn my mother that somebody is spreading rumors about-- about her. Somebody was helping us dig that hole. So I think they suspected. I don't think the peasant knew, the one that-- I don't know. I had some-- you know, I had to pretend to be a railroad person, and the railroad people drank a lot in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they-- they'd-- so they would ask me to go and have some vodka with them. And I made a deal. I knew I couldn't drink. So I made a deal with the woman who had the concession that whenever I came with somebody she should pour or give me water. Because they drink vodka in a glass, just straight. So they-- so she was very happy, and she would serve the-- the water to me. And they would drink, and I got the reputation that I really can hold my whiskey. I forgot that it was water. And when I came to this country, vodka was just becoming popular. And we went to a party. And somebody asked me what-- you know, the host was so pleased. He had vodka and whatnot, what will I have. I said, vodka. And he said, how do you want it? I said, what do you mean how I want it? Straight. I still had this idea that I really can hold my-- my liquor. And he gave me a little vodka. You even fooled yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I drank it. And-- and boy, did I get drunk on [LAUGHS] that one shot. So the big joke was, you know, oh, Edith's the one who can hold her liquor. [LAUGHS] But it's funny how you forget certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Edith, is there anything else you'd like to tell us about that-- that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time there was a train coming from Warsaw with Jews. And it stopped at the station. And people were looking out these tiny windows with the wires in front. You know, some of them had tin cups. They were begging for waters-- and for water-- and I was scared silly that they would recognize me. And yet, I wanted to know who was on-- on the train. And they organized, like two other women, that help me. And the train stopped maybe for half an hour or so. So there was a pump. And we had the bucket, actually more. We have three buckets. So I-- I was pumping the water and giving the women the bucket. And they were going around along the train and filling up these little cups, you know, that they-- some people had. And I remember just looking like, over my shoulder every so often, you know, to see if I glimpse somebody that-- that I knew. And yet, I was scared that they might recognize-- somebody might recognize me and yell out. And so-- and one time I also saved-- one woman, she was brought into the office. And the guy there, one of these German people, said-- I don't know who brought her in, if it was a Polish-- Polish railroad man or who. But this one German railroad man said he had to call the [NON-ENGLISH] or the [NON-ENGLISH]. And he couldn't get through. And then he said, you call. And he walked out, and he walked out. And she was sitting there. And I recognized she was Jewish, although she wouldn't admit it. But, you know, I knew that she was Jewish. And she was just terrified. She was sitting there. But there was nobody in the office but me and her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I told her, go, you know. And she looks up, and I say, go. And so she went, and I even told her a street where I knew she could maybe hide. Because there was some-- some little-- like a little forest. I said, go to your right and then-- and go. And then the German, the railroad man came in. And-- well, maybe 15 minutes later. And he says, what happened? Did you call? And I said, well I don't know, that I had to go to the bathroom. So he said, what do you mean you had to go to a bathroom? Don't you know we called [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, maybe the [NON-ENGLISH] came. You called and I called, you know. So he says, you know, if-- if she escape, we both are in trouble. So I said, so let's not say anything. So he said, OK. Well, let's forget the whole story. [LAUGHS] So we forgot the whole story. Anyway, I was liberated 1944 by the-- by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Otwock when I was liberated. And I was with my-- my parents came from Warsaw again after this way, by blackmail. And I was hiding my father under the bed. Because still-- the Germans were still there. So it was a rough time. [SIGHS] But the Russians came in. And we-- I mean, you know, we were under attack with artillery and the bombing, and it was war. And [LAUGHS] one morning we-- my father went out, and here was a Russian just [LAUGHS] walking. And so then after that, we went to Lublin. And in Lublin, I got a job first, at work I got a job at the-- with the government, the provisional government. Because again, I knew languages. I knew a bit-- at that time I knew English, and I knew German and Polish. So it was like the information-- information department, and my job was to listen to the radio, and say what I heard. [SIGHS] I remember listening to the BBC. But I didn't work there long. Because my father wanted me out of that government. You know, it was a communist government, and he didn't want me to work there. And then they opened the medical school, the [NON-ENGLISH] University Medical School. And I was able to get in. As I mentioned, I had a very good background. I finished my lyceum in-- in the ghetto, actually. And I passed some exams. And I knew a teacher, a professor who was in the ghetto. And he knew about the secret courses, the clandestine courses. And I got in. And I studied for-- for one year. I mean up till June. And then-- and the war ended in May. And then I learned that the United Nations are supporting some DP students in Germany, that they are planning or something to start to support them. And so a group of us left. We went to Germany, illegally through really walking most, and with some horrid experiences with some Russians-- not actually Russians-- the Poles were worse. On the border, they were supposed to be bribed and let us through. So they robbed us. And then when we were on top of a little bridge, they put all the floodlights on us, and they started shooting, but not really at us. But we didn't know, you see, so we had to run. And then we ran right into [LAUGHS] some Russians. And so it was quite-- you know, very-- a lot of experiences. But finally, we went through the Russian zone, through the British zone, and finally I make my way to Wiesbaden. And then I-- I think it was Wiesbaden. Yeah, Wiesbaden. And then we were assigned to a university in Marburg, in Germany. And we started to study medicine. Now, I already had two semesters behind me. So I was the more advanced of the group. We had a group of-- I forgot-- maybe 14 Jewish students. But there were other DP students. There were other-- there was some Lithuanians, some-- we didn't have any Poles. They went back, but Ukrainians, a Yugoslavian, I remember. And we all lived in this one building that was a student home. And we studied at the university. And in-- 1947, I got the B'nai B'rith scholarship. And I came to Hillel, B'nai B'rith, and I came to the University of Kentucky. And-- I-- I studied. And that's where I met my husband. But Kentucky, at that time, didn't have any-- at that time didn't have any medical school. So I studied bacteriology. And my husband was a GI studying on his GI bill. And-- he was-- and after the war, he was in Europe during the war. And so we met in Kentucky. We got married. And-- I mean, we didn't get married then. We got married a little later. Anyway, then afterwards we moved to Chicago. I worked as a research serologist in Chicago. My husband got his PhD, and-- at Northwestern University. And then we moved to-- to Long Island, New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have children-- children, Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had two children, one a daughter. And she's a lawyer in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Diane. And a son, Steven, who is getting his PhD in psychology now. He was an engineer. And he decided he wanted to work with people. So he switched, and he's finishing his PhD. And I just became grandma this year, after all these years. I'm 72 years old. And I never thought I would be grandma. But my daughter surprised us. And finally [LAUGHS] got married at the age of 40, and had a baby a year later. And a month later, my daughter-in-law had a little girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: How wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you then moved with your husband to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I moved first to Chicago. And my husband-- and then to New York, and then came here. My husband is a scientist, worked for the Fox Chase Cancer Center. And I'm very proud of him. Because he is the one who worked on the-- discovered-- made the hepatitis B vaccine. So he has a patent on that. And he was inducted in the National-- National Academy of-- gee-- National Inventors Hall of Fame. [LAUGHS] That's what it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he is-- and we also-- the man that he worked with, Dr. Blumberg, got the Nobel Prize in 1976. And since my husband was intimately, you know, involved in this in this research, we also got invited. And that was the highlight of my life, after the war is to go to Stockholm for the-- the Nobel Prize. We're almost at the end of this tape. We'll-- we'll continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: More? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well--
